data.title: Brown And Brown (BRO) Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Earnings Growth Narrative In Q1 2026
data.description:
Brown & Brown (BRO) kicked off Q1 2026 with total revenue of US$1.9 billion and basic EPS of US$1.27, alongside trailing twelve month figures of US$6.3 billion in revenue and US$3.53 in EPS that frame the latest quarter in a bigger context. Over recent periods, revenue has moved from US$1.2 billion in Q4 2024 to US$1.6 billion in Q4 2025 and US$1.9 billion in Q1 2026. Quarterly basic EPS has shifted from US$0.73 to US$0.78 and now US$1.27, setting up a story where investors will be weighing earnings power against how margins are holding up.

See our full analysis for Brown & Brown.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the widely followed growth, risk, and valuation narratives around Brown & Brown.

See what the community is saying about Brown & Brown

Advertisement

Net income and margins cooling from prior year

  • On a trailing basis, Brown & Brown earned US$1.1b of net income on US$6.3b of revenue, which works out to an 18.2% net profit margin compared with 21% in the prior year.
  • Analysts' consensus view points to earnings stability supported by cost discipline and debt repayment, yet the 18.2% trailing margin versus 21% a year ago and quarterly net income of US$421 million versus US$327 million in Q1 2025 shows that margin compression is sitting alongside higher profit dollars.
    • Consensus commentary highlights an improved EBITDAC margin by 110 basis points and reduced interest expense, while the reported net margin has moved lower year on year, so efficiency gains are being partially offset elsewhere in the income statement.
    • The same consensus view talks about revenue opportunities in employee benefits and CAT property, but the current 18.2% net margin suggests that any benefit from those areas is not fully flowing through to the bottom line yet.

Multi year earnings growth vs slower outlook

  • Over the past five years, earnings grew at 15.9% per year, while trailing 12 month earnings growth sits at 11.6% and forecasts point to around 8.2% earnings growth and 8.3% revenue growth per year.
  • Analysts' consensus narrative leans positive on future expansion through acquisitions and strong demand in employee benefits, but the moderation from 15.9% historical earnings growth to 11.6% recently and about 8.2% expected highlights that the growth story is becoming more gradual than the past run rate.
    • The company completed 13 acquisitions with projected annual revenue of US$36 million, which is small compared with US$6.3b of trailing revenue, so these deals may fine tune growth more than transform it.
    • Consensus expects ongoing demand for benefits consulting and broader revenue expansion, yet the forecast 8.3% revenue growth remains below broader US market forecasts cited, so investors are being asked to pay attention to quality of growth rather than sheer speed.
Brown & Brown's earnings path tells only part of the story, and many investors are watching how the community weighs growth, margins, and valuation in one place, especially after this set of numbers. See what the community is saying about Brown & Brown.

P/E premium and DCF gap to current price

  • At a share price of US$63.14, the trailing P/E of 18.8x sits above the US Insurance industry average of 11.6x but below the peer group average of 35.7x, while a DCF fair value of US$138.64 and an analyst target of US$78.33 both stand above the current price.
  • Consensus narrative notes that effective cost management and cash generation help support the case for this valuation, yet the premium P/E, 18.2% net margin that is lower than last year, and balance sheet flags around debt coverage and shareholder dilution all mean the market is weighing mixed signals rather than a one way quality story.
    • On the risk side, debt is described as not well covered by operating cash flow and shareholders were diluted over the past year, so the balance sheet is an active part of the discussion, not an afterthought.
    • On the reward side, the model DCF fair value of US$138.64 and analyst target of US$78.33 both sit above US$63.14, which contrasts with the softer margin profile and slower growth forecasts and helps explain why opinions on the current P/E premium are likely divided.

With sentiment clearly mixed between margin pressure, valuation signals, and growth expectations, this is a moment to move fast and check the numbers for yourself rather than rely on a single view. Approach 1: Atlanticus Holdings Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company generates above the return that equity investors require, then links that to the value of its equity per share. Instead of focusing on near term earnings alone, it asks whether the business earns more than its estimated cost of equity over time.

For Atlanticus Holdings, the starting point is a Book Value of $40.79 per share and a Stable EPS estimate of $8.75 per share, based on the median return on equity from the past 5 years. The model applies a Cost of Equity of $5.57 per share, which implies an Excess Return of $3.18 per share. That excess is supported by an Average Return on Equity of 19.36% and a Stable Book Value estimate of $45.19 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from 2 analysts.

Putting these inputs together, the Excess Returns model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $80.78 per share. Compared with the recent share price of about $79.39, this suggests the stock is roughly 1.7% undervalued, which is a very small gap.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Atlanticus Holdings.

Approach 2: Atlanticus Holdings Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. This makes it a practical cross check against more complex models like excess returns or discounted cash flow work.

What counts as a “normal” P/E ratio depends on how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while lower growth or higher risk usually line up with a lower multiple.

Atlanticus Holdings currently trades on a P/E of 10.59x. That sits above the Consumer Finance industry average P/E of 9.33x and the peer group average of 5.64x. data.title: Is Now The Time To Put Nagahori (TSE:8139) On Your Watchlist?
data.description:
Investors are often guided by the idea of discovering 'the next big thing', even if that means buying 'story stocks' without any revenue, let alone profit. But as Peter Lynch said in One Up On Wall Street, 'Long shots almost never pay off.' While a well funded company may sustain losses for years, it will need to generate a profit eventually, or else investors will move on and the company will wither away.

In contrast to all that, many investors prefer to focus on companies like Nagahori (TSE:8139), which has not only revenues, but also profits. While profit isn't the sole metric that should be considered when investing, it's worth recognising businesses that can consistently produce it.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Nagahori's Improving Profits

In business, profits are a key measure of success; and share prices tend to reflect earnings per share (EPS) performance. Which is why EPS growth is looked upon so favourably. Commendations have to be given in seeing that Nagahori grew its EPS from JP¥18.58 to JP¥61.43, in one short year. Even though that growth rate may not be repeated, that looks like a breakout improvement. But the key is discerning whether something profound has changed, or if this is a just a one-off boost.

One way to double-check a company's growth is to look at how its revenue, and earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins are changing. EBIT margins for Nagahori remained fairly unchanged over the last year, however the company should be pleased to report its revenue growth for the period of 18% to JP¥27b. That's a real positive.

The chart below shows how the company's bottom and top lines have progressed over time. To see the actual numbers, click on the chart.

Check out our latest analysis for Nagahori

Since Nagahori is no giant, with a market capitalisation of JP¥31b, you should definitely check its cash and debt before getting too excited about its prospects.

Are Nagahori Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

It's pleasing to see company leaders with putting their money on the line, so to speak, because it increases alignment of incentives between the people running the business, and its true owners. So it is good to see that Nagahori insiders have a significant amount of capital invested in the stock. Indeed, they hold JP¥7.5b worth of its stock. This considerable investment should help drive long-term value in the business. That amounts to 24% of the company, demonstrating a degree of high-level alignment with shareholders.

Does Nagahori Deserve A Spot On Your Watchlist?

Nagahori's earnings per share growth have been climbing higher at an appreciable rate. That sort of growth is nothing short of eye-catching, and the large investment held by insiders should certainly brighten the view of the company. At times fast EPS growth is a sign the business has reached an inflection point, so there's a potential opportunity to be had here. So at the surface level, Nagahori is worth putting on your watchlist; after all, shareholders do well when the market underestimates fast growing companies. Still, you should learn about the 1 warning sign we've spotted with Nagahori.

data.description:

  • The stock last closed at US$323.78, with a 7 day return of a 2.5% decline, a 30 day return of 3.2%, a year to date return of a 6.0% decline, and a 1 year return of a 4.9% decline, set against a 3 year return of 2.4% and a 5 year return of 33.1%.

Approach 1: Aon Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to generate above the return that shareholders require, then converts those surplus profits into an intrinsic value per share.

For Aon, the model starts with a Book Value of US$43.60 per share and a Stable EPS of US$24.31 per share, based on the median return on equity from the past 5 years. The implied Average Return on Equity is 43.28%, compared with a Cost of Equity of US$4.17 per share. This leaves an Excess Return of US$20.14 per share. Analysts also point to a Stable Book Value of US$56.17 per share, based on weighted future book value estimates from three analysts.

Using these inputs in the Excess Returns framework gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$558.73 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$323.78, this implies the stock is about 42.1% undervalued on this model.

Approach 2: Aon Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. This makes it a useful cross check against cash flow based estimates.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected earnings growth and lower perceived risk tend to support higher P/E levels, while lower growth or higher risk usually line up with lower P/E ratios.

Aon currently trades on a P/E of 18.77x. That is above the Insurance industry average P/E of 11.62x, yet below the peer group average of 24.09x, so the stock is priced somewhere between broad sector levels and closer peers.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Aon is 12.68x. This is a proprietary estimate of what Aon’s P/E might be given its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market value and company specific risks. Set against the current P/E of 18.77x, the Fair Ratio of 12.68x indicates that Aon is trading on a richer multiple than this model would suggest.
